video_title: Eliot Higgins: How Bellingcat Hunts Down the Truth | Podcast | In Good Company
gist: Bellingcat founder Elliot Higgins explains how open-source investigation (geolocation, satellite imagery, Russia's for-sale government data) cracked the MH17, Skripal, and Navalny cases, and argues AI's biggest current effect on truth is giving people a 'permission structure' to dismiss real evidence as fake, inside an engagement-driven information ecosystem that structurally rewards disinformation over verification.

insights.claim: Bellingcat's entire investigative model rests on 'open source investigation' -- using publicly available material like social media posts, satellite imagery, and marine-traffic or flight-tracking data -- a data surface founder Elliot Higgins says has 'mushroomed' over the last 15 years, expanding what a well-organized group of amateurs can independently verify.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: The growing volume of investigable public data is a structural tailwind for any organization that builds systematic OSINT capability rather than relying solely on official or media sources.
insights.quote: So, that's using publicly available material to investigate a range of different topics.

insights.claim: Bellingcat's signature 'geolocation' technique -- matching video content to satellite imagery to identify exactly where footage was filmed -- originated in 2012 when Elliot Higgins, then an untrained blogger, began cataloguing YouTube videos from the Syrian war.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: A now-standard verification technique in conflict and disinformation analysis traces to one amateur's persistence, showing how low the barrier is to building high-value OSINT capability from scratch.
insights.quote: I realized you could use satellite imagery to figure out where stuff was filmed. And we call this geoloccation.

insights.claim: Bellingcat runs a three-tier structure: about 35 core staff (mostly researchers and trainers), roughly 200 trained volunteer investigators it can direct, and a self-organizing Discord community of about 43,000 people that surfaces leads the core team isn't even looking at.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: A case study in how a lean paid core team can generate outsized investigative output by training and coordinating a much larger unpaid network.
insights.quote: There's also a Discord server where we have lots of people. It's kind of our community server that has about 43,000 people on it.

insights.claim: Bellingcat's core operating principle is to never prejudge a conclusion -- evidence is gathered and verified first, opinions form only afterward -- which Higgins frames as the key differentiator from partisan online communities that pick a side and then hunt for confirming 'evidence.'
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: An evidence-first discipline that is a transferable analytical framework for any research process, including investment diligence, trying to avoid confirmation bias.
insights.quote: It's you never prejudge a situation. Just because you feel something should be the truth doesn't mean it actually is the truth.

insights.claim: Bellingcat's crowdsourced volunteer research after the July 2014 downing of Malaysia Airlines flight MH17 uncovered details -- including the origin point from which the missile launcher had launched -- that no other organization, official or otherwise, had found.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: Shows OSINT can out-perform state intelligence services on hard factual questions when applied at volunteer scale and speed.
insights.quote: that led us to finding all kinds of stuff that no one else anywhere had found like the origin of the missile launcher where it had launched from.

insights.claim: Bellingcat identified the two Russian officers behind the 2018 Skripal poisoning in Salisbury by exploiting Russia's 'gray data market,' where nearly all government records (passport files, car and house registrations) are purchasable if you know who to pay, after noticing the suspects' passport numbers were only a few digits apart.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: A durable structural weakness -- corrupt, for-sale Russian government data -- has repeatedly exposed state operations and is worth monitoring as a source of future disclosures.
insights.quote: their passport numbers were just a few digits apart, which is very suspicious.

insights.claim: In investigating the 2020 poisoning of Alexei Navalny, Bellingcat purchased Russian phone records showing the FSB chemical-weapons team's cell-tower pings, letting investigators reconstruct the agents' movements across Europe and Russia minute by minute.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: Demonstrates a repeatable forensic method -- cell-tower triangulation via purchased records -- for tracking state actors that other researchers could apply to similar cases.
insights.quote: the phone records aren't just who you've called, it's every single cell phone tower you connect to, which can then be mapped out.

insights.claim: A Bellingcat colleague, posing as an FSB officer over a spoofed phone number, got one of Navalny's actual poisoners to describe on tape exactly how the operation was carried out -- poison applied to the seam of his underpants that activated when he dressed for the airport -- directly contradicting Putin's public denial days earlier.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: A vivid example of open-source and social-engineering tactics combining to produce confession-grade evidence that overturned an official state denial within days.
insights.quote: you put um poison on the seam of his underpants, and then when he put them on and went to the airport, it activated

insights.claim: Higgins says Russian retaliation against Bellingcat has escalated since 2015 from state-media smear campaigns (RT, Sputnik) to hacking attempts and, most recently, the jailing of six Bulgarian nationals convicted of spying on a Bellingcat colleague for two years on behalf of the FSB.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: Signals that Russian state security services treat open-source investigators as high-priority targets, relevant to assessing operational and personnel risk for any organization publishing sensitive Russia-related research.
insights.quote: most recently there's been six Bulgarians who were jailed because they were spying on one of my colleagues for 2 years and that was linked to the uh Russian FSB.

insights.claim: As a result of his work, Higgins is treated as a high-value security target -- UK counterterrorism police from the West and East Midlands periodically visit him to brief him on relevant threats -- requiring him to constantly distinguish ordinary 'weirdness' from targeted surveillance.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: A reminder that high-impact investigative work carries persistent personal-security costs that scale with the target's public profile.
insights.quote: at the moment I have West Midlands or East Midland's counterterrorism police uh pay me the occasional visit to let me know what's going on in their world, which is always a bit disconcerting.

insights.claim: Higgins says he can no longer safely travel to the United States because Bellingcat has been swept into the 'censorship industrial complex' conspiracy theory alleging collusion between the Biden administration, academics and journalists, and because Elon Musk has publicly accused Bellingcat of being a government asset, raising his perceived risk of detention at a US airport.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: Shows how US political conspiracy narratives and prominent tech and political figures can materially constrain the operations of an independent research organization with no documented ties to the alleged conspiracy.
insights.quote: Elon Musk called us a scop.

themes.theme: AI and the 'Permission Structure' for Denying Reality

insights.claim: Higgins argues AI's biggest current effect on the information environment isn't generating convincing fakes but giving people a 'permission structure' to dismiss real, verified evidence they dislike by simply claiming -- often without proof -- that it's AI-generated.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: For any market participant relying on visual or video evidence to inform decisions, this predicts rising 'reflexive denial' friction even when the underlying evidence is genuine and verified.
insights.quote: I think AI gives people a permission structure to deny my reality.

insights.claim: A fully AI-generated far-right UK rapper -- fake music, face, and persona -- still achieved real audience engagement, because people engaged with content they wanted to be true rather than content that was factual, illustrating that 'truthiness' now outcompetes factual accuracy for attention.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: Evidence that synthetic-media engagement is decoupled from authenticity, relevant to modeling platform engagement economics or reputational risk from AI-generated content.
insights.quote: there was a far-right UK rapper who was entirely AI generated. The music, the face, everything was fake, but lots of people engaged with it

insights.claim: Despite experimenting with AI extensively, Higgins says it hasn't yet materially changed Bellingcat's core investigative process -- its clearest practical use so far is coding and automation (Claude wrote a scraping script that finished an 8-year-delayed task in 10 minutes), while AI-assisted geolocation produces only 'mixed' results and still needs manual verification.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: A grounded, first-hand counterpoint to AI-transforms-everything narratives: for OSINT-adjacent workflows, current-generation AI is a productivity tool for coding and automation, not yet a reliable analytical replacement.
insights.quote: It wrote me a Python script and within 10 minutes it had done a job that I'd been putting off for eight years.

insights.claim: A large-scale Russian disinformation campaign targeting Armenia -- 'hundreds of videos, fakes, all sorts' -- generated almost no engagement because English-speaking social media audiences had no pre-existing interest in Armenian politics, showing that disinformation requires an audience already motivated to engage, not just volume of fake content.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: Disinformation risk should be assessed by audience receptivity to a narrative, not sheer campaign volume, useful for gauging which fake narratives are actually likely to move opinion or markets.
insights.quote: for disinformation to work, you need people who want to engage with it.

insights.claim: Higgins frames functioning democracy as resting on three pillars -- verification (establishing what's true), deliberation (deciding what to do about it), and accountability (holding power to account) -- and argues all three are degrading into performative, loyalty-signaling substitutes where truth is secondary to demonstrating in-group allegiance.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: A reusable framework for assessing institutional and governance quality in any country -- screen for which of the three pillars (verification, deliberation, accountability) is failing when evaluating political risk.
insights.quote: verification. How do we know what's true?

insights.claim: Higgins says the information system has shifted from a top-down, institutionally-verified model to a peer-to-peer, many-to-many system where platforms like X monetize engagement over accuracy, structurally favoring populist, authoritarian, and conspiratorial content because it doesn't require procedural verification to spread.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: A structural, platform-economics explanation for why misinformation persistently outcompetes verified reporting, relevant to reputational and narrative risk around any engagement-optimized platform.
insights.quote: That's now collapsed into a peer-to-peer manyto many information system where institutions are competing for attention with the public in systems that rewards engagement over the truth.

insights.claim: Higgins calls the post-2016 consensus that disinformation is primarily a 'foreign interference' supply-side problem 'complete nonsense' -- he argues the real driver is public distrust of institutions pushing people into engagement-optimized online spaces that were never designed for truth-seeking.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: Implies policy responses focused solely on removing foreign disinformation supply will underperform; institutional distrust on the demand side is the more durable variable to watch.
insights.quote: Problem is that's complete nonsense.

insights.claim: US foreign-aid funding cuts eliminated an estimated 25-40% of global funding for investigative-journalism NGOs virtually overnight, according to Higgins, even though Bellingcat itself took no US aid money -- a sector-wide funding shock that hit outlets in the Global South hardest.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: A quantifiable, sudden supply-side shock to the independent-journalism and OSINT ecosystem -- fewer investigative outlets globally means less scrutiny of state and corporate actors going forward.
insights.quote: about 25 to 40% of the global funding for the kind of you investigative journalism NOS's was lost overnight because of those US aid funding cuts.

insights.claim: Bellingcat's funding evolved from 2014 Kickstarter crowdfunding to a mix of foundational funders, workshops (about 30% of income), and small donations, and is now shifting further toward high-net-worth individual donors as foundations redirect money to keep other collapsing NGOs afloat after the US aid cuts.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: Shows how philanthropic capital reallocation after a funding shock creates knock-on effects even for organizations not directly dependent on the cut funding source.
insights.quote: we raise about 30% of our income from workshops

themes.theme: Signal Value of Verified Information for Decision-Makers

insights.claim: Higgins argues that when 'reality becomes contested' -- citing the fast-shifting, hard-to-verify narrative around the Iran conflict as an example -- it becomes very difficult to make tactical or strategic decisions, which is why Bellingcat has trained investment firms, alongside journalists and NGOs, in open-source investigation techniques.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: Direct signal that institutional investors are already building internal OSINT capability to compensate for unreliable official narratives on geopolitical flashpoints, a capability edge worth benchmarking against.
insights.quote: it's very hard to make any real you know tactical strategic decisions about things and this is where open source investigation is so valuable.

insights.claim: On the Iran conflict, Higgins says there is abundant information but it is mostly 'very poor quality' and untrustworthy, forcing Bellingcat to fall back on techniques like infrared satellite-imagery differencing to build damage heat maps when standard visual satellite and social-media data were unavailable due to Iran's internet shutdown.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: A concrete alternative-data method -- infrared or thermal satellite differencing -- for assessing conflict-zone damage and activity when conventional visual and social feeds go dark, relevant to anyone tracking Iran-related tail risk.
insights.quote: there's lots of information it's just very poor quality information that you can't trust.

insights.claim: Bellingcat found and published video of Tomahawk missiles striking near, not on, an Iranian school building within about 48 hours of the strike, which let journalists press President Trump on the discrepancy; he responded by claiming 'the Iranians have Tomahawk missiles as well,' an answer Higgins calls 'completely ridiculous' but says was forced by Bellingcat's verifiable evidence.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: A concrete, recent example of open-source evidence directly constraining a sitting US president's public statements on an active Iran conflict, a template for how OSINT can move accountability in real time.
insights.quote: we found a video of Tomahawk missiles striking close to that building but not on it.

insights.claim: Higgins says investigators have 'at best two or three hours' to get verified evidence into public discourse during a fast-breaking event, because once people settle on a belief about what happened they rarely revise it, and official or government narratives will otherwise fill the vacuum first.
insights.speaker: Elliot Higgins
insights.so_what: A concrete timing benchmark for how fast credible counter-narratives must be published to compete with first-mover, often state-driven, narratives after breaking events.
insights.quote: I think you've got at best two or three hours in many cases.
